title: Supreme Court Blocks Trump on Birthright Citizenship, US-Iran Talks Hit a Wall in Doha, North American Trade Deal Stalls
subhead: The US Supreme Court ends its term with a landmark birthright citizenship ruling and expanded executive power. Meanwhile, US-Iran negotiations falter as envoys meet mediators but not each other, the USMCA deadline passes without a deal, and Pakistan-Afghanistan border violence escalates.

sections.heading: Supreme Court Upholds Birthright Citizenship, Expands Presidential Power
sections.body:
The US Supreme Court ruled 6-3 that children born on US soil are constitutionally entitled to citizenship regardless of their parents' immigration status, rejecting President Trump's executive order that sought to end the 150-year-old practice. Chief Justice John Roberts wrote that the 14th Amendment's promise of citizenship extends to 'every free-born person in this land.' Justices Thomas, Gorsuch, and Alito dissented, with Alito calling the ruling a 'serious mistake.' Trump responded on Truth Social that the decision was 'too bad' and urged Congress to act legislatively — a path with slim chances in the Senate, as BBC and multiple outlets noted.

But the birthright ruling was the exception, not the pattern, of this term. The court's conservative majority methodically expanded executive power: on Monday, six conservatives ruled Trump can dismiss members of independent regulatory agencies based on policy disagreements — giving the president sweeping influence over labour, election, communications, environmental and financial regulation. The court also upheld bans on transgender athletes in female school and college sports (9-0 on Title IX, 6-3 on constitutional grounds), loosened campaign finance restrictions, and struck down racial minority voting district protections under the 1965 Voting Rights Act. As University of Pennsylvania law professor Kate Shaw told BBC, this is 'the broadest conception of presidential power that we have ever seen.'
sections.whyItMatters: The term's contradictory pattern — curbing Trump on birthright and tariffs, while broadening presidential authority over regulators, elections, and immigration enforcement — reshapes the balance of power in ways that will outlast any single president.

sections.heading: US-Iran: Envoys in Doha, But No Direct Talks Scheduled
sections.body:
US special envoy Steve Witkoff and senior adviser Jared Kushner arrived in Doha on Tuesday, but Qatar's foreign ministry spokesman Majed al-Ansari confirmed there are no high-level meetings or direct talks scheduled between the US and Iranian sides. The envoys are meeting mediators to discuss 'all regional issues,' including Lebanon, but not Iranian officials. Iran's foreign ministry spokesman Esmail Baqai likewise stated that 'no meeting at any level with the American side has been scheduled for the coming days.'

The gap between the two positions remains wide. A US official said Sunday that both sides would 'stand down' and that vessels could 'move freely' through the Strait of Hormuz, but Iran's lead technical negotiator Kazem Gharibabadi denied that technical talks were planned for this week. Iran continues to insist it will 'do whatever is necessary to safeguard its interests' over Hormuz. The $6bn tranche of $12bn in frozen Iranian assets held in Qatar remains contingent on progress in talks that have not yet happened, Ansari confirmed.
sections.whyItMatters: The US-Iran MOU is barely two weeks old and already fraying. Both sides claim the other is stalling, technical talks have been publicly disputed, and the key economic incentive — unfreezing Iranian assets — remains locked. Without progress, the ceasefires hold only by force of external mediation.

sections.heading: USMCA Deadline Passes Without a Deal as US, Canada, Mexico Head for Annual Reviews
sections.body:
The July 1 deadline for the US, Canada, and Mexico to decide on renewing the USMCA trade pact came and went without agreement. Trade representatives from all three countries met formally on Wednesday for the first time since the review process began, but Canadian Prime Minister Mark Carney told reporters he was 'not looking for my pen' — no deal is imminent. The US is not expected to renew the deal, setting the stage for rolling annual reviews until the pact's 2036 expiry date, according to Reuters and the Globe and Mail.

President Trump has said he sees the USMCA 'expiring immediately,' while also saying he is 'open' to leaving it in place. The deal underpins nearly $1.6tn in annual trilateral trade. Canada's main sticking point is US tariffs on steel, aluminium, and automobiles. The US is demanding greater access to Canada's dairy market and the reversal of provincial boycotts on US alcohol. US ambassador Pete Hoekstra said a near-deal was derailed last October after Ontario ran anti-tariff ads on American networks that angered Trump. The USMCA's exemption currently shields most Canadian and Mexican goods from the bulk of Trump's tariffs — without it, those tariffs snap back.
sections.whyItMatters: Annual reviews replace a stable 16-year extension, injecting permanent uncertainty into North American supply chains. The longer this drags on, the more businesses face planning paralysis — and the more leverage the US retains by keeping the threat of withdrawal alive.

sections.heading: Pakistan-Afghanistan Border Escalates as Taliban Strikes Back
sections.body:
Afghanistan's Taliban government said it carried out strikes on targets along the border with Pakistan, with several people reportedly injured in Pakistan's southwestern Balochistan province. Pakistan's military said it shot down four rudimentary drones and warned that any further provocation 'would receive a befitting response.' The exchange comes two days after Pakistan launched its own airstrikes on Afghanistan, which the UN said killed 28 civilians. Afghanistan put its civilian death toll at 36, with over 160 injured, calling the Pakistani strikes a 'cowardly act' and an 'atrocity.'

Tensions had been relatively calm since a ceasefire in October, but the pattern is now one of rapid escalation. Pakistan accuses Afghanistan of harbouring militants who carry out attacks on Pakistani soil — a claim the Taliban rejects. Pakistan's information minister Attaullah Tarar said 29 militants were killed in Sunday's operation. The BBC has not independently confirmed figures from either side.
sections.whyItMatters: Two nuclear-armed neighbours are trading air strikes and drone attacks on a border that was quiet just weeks ago. Each side's civilian casualty claims harden domestic political pressure to respond, making de-escalation harder with each exchange.

sections.heading: Venezuela Quake Toll Tops 1,700 as Rescuers Pull Survivors From Rubble
sections.body:
More than 1,700 people have been killed in Venezuela's twin earthquakes, interim president Delcy Rodríguez said, calling it the country's 'most brutal natural catastrophe' in history. The magnitude 7.2 and 7.5 quakes struck the northern state of La Guaira last Wednesday within 39 seconds of each other, collapsing nearly 800 buildings. A 4.6-magnitude aftershock on Monday frayed nerves but caused no further damage.

Rescue efforts are still largely volunteer-driven. The BBC saw residents in La Guaira using crowbars, mallets, and bare hands to search rubble. A 21-year-old man was pulled alive on Monday after over 100 hours trapped, found by teams from Venezuela, Mexico, and El Salvador. The UN said more than 500 aftershocks have hit the region, at least 2,500 structures have been affected, and it is obtaining 10,000 body bags. The US has pledged over $300m in aid, and the US military has repaired and reopened the Port of La Guaira to speed up deliveries, with the USS Fort Lauderdale currently docked. China has pledged nearly $15m. Venezuela's dire pre-existing economic crisis has hampered the government response, with residents openly criticising the slow deployment of heavy equipment.
sections.whyItMatters: The death toll is almost certainly still rising, and Venezuela's economic collapse means the country cannot rebuild alone. The arrival of US military assets for humanitarian aid creates an unusual dynamic between Caracas and Washington — one where both need each other despite deep political hostility.

sections.heading: Beijing Plane Crash Exposes Security Gaps, China Imposes Information Blackout
sections.body:
A small Chinese-made Aurora SA60L aircraft slammed into Beijing's 109-storey CITIC Tower on Friday, killing the pilot and wounding 13 others. Four days later, China has published only a 60-word official statement. Dramatic footage and even unrelated photos of the skyscraper — a local cultural landmark — have been scrubbed from Chinese social media. At least three aviation firms told the BBC they were told to suspend light aircraft operations and not discuss the incident.

The crash happened just a few kilometres from Zhongnanhai, the Communist Party's headquarters, raising serious questions about Beijing's air defence. China analyst Bill Bishop called it a 'massive security breach,' noting that with a few more seconds of flight time the aircraft could have reached the leadership compound. Raymond Kuo of the Chicago Council on Global Affairs compared it to Mathias Rust's 1987 flight into Red Square, which led to the dismissal of senior Soviet military officials. The censorship speed and thoroughness suggest Beijing's leadership is still unsure what happened — and is worried about what that uncertainty implies for its security apparatus.
sections.whyItMatters: If a small civilian aircraft can penetrate Beijing's airspace to within kilometres of the party's headquarters, the implications for China's air defence systems are serious. The information blackout is not just about public panic — it reflects a leadership genuinely uncertain about its own security vulnerabilities.

bottomLine: July 1 brought three developments that will shape the weeks ahead: the US Supreme Court upheld birthright citizenship but handed Trump broader regulatory and electoral power in a term that expanded presidential authority to historic levels; US-Iran talks stalled in Doha with neither side willing to meet directly, leaving the fragile Hormuz ceasefire without a diplomatic anchor; and the USMCA's July 1 deadline passed without a deal, consigning North American trade to annual uncertainty. Meanwhile, Pakistan and Afghanistan are exchanging air strikes, Venezuela's earthquake toll has surpassed 1,700 with recovery still dependent on volunteers, and Beijing's silence over a plane crash near its leadership compound speaks louder than any official statement.
